https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.22440.authors: Sharique Hasan, Alexander Oettl, Sampsa Samila
https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.22440.centre_angle: This paper argues that deploying LLMs does not eliminate complexity but redistributes it — from the user interface to organizational infrastructure, compliance, and specialist roles. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, this reframes the central managerial challenge: the question is not whether to adopt AI but how to design the internal structures that absorb the complexity AI ostensibly hides. This aligns with findings from CAMO's Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index, which identifies organizational and executional barriers — siloed departments, poor data quality, lack of quick wins — as the primary reasons AI initiatives fall short of expected returns; the GAS framework offers a theoretical account of why those barriers are structurally inevitable rather than merely implementation failures.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.22440.claude_summary: When firms deploy large language models, complexity does not disappear — it relocates. This paper introduces a Generality-Accuracy-Simplicity (GAS) framework to show that LLMs' apparently simple interfaces shift hidden costs onto organizational infrastructure, compliance functions, and specialist staff. The authors argue that competitive advantage no longer comes from adopting AI but from managing this redistributed complexity through deliberate decisions about abstraction layers, workflow design, and complementary human expertise. Treating AI purely as an input-cost reduction misses both the inherent trade-offs among generality, accuracy, and simplicity, and the organizational burden created when user-facing simplicity is achieved by pushing complexity inward.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.22440.key_takeaway: AI does not eliminate organizational complexity — it relocates it to infrastructure, compliance, and specialist staff, making the design of internal workflows and expertise the true source of competitive advantage.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.22440.linkedin_caption_draft:
AI looks simple from the outside. Inside the organization, it is anything but.

A new paper from Hasan, Oettl, and Samila introduces the Generality-Accuracy-Simplicity (GAS) framework to explain why: when LLMs offer broad capability through easy interfaces, the underlying complexity does not vanish — it shifts to infrastructure, compliance, and specialist personnel inside the firm.

The implication for strategy is direct. Competitive advantage no longer comes from being an early adopter. It comes from mastering the redistribution — designing abstraction layers, aligning workflows, and building the human expertise that makes AI outputs reliable in high-stakes contexts.

CAMO's own survey of C-suite executives in China and Hong Kong found exactly this: the dominant barriers to AI value are organizational and executional, not technical. This paper offers a framework for understanding why that pattern is structural.

For leaders planning AI investments: where is the complexity landing in your organization, and who is responsible for absorbing it?

#AIStrategy #AIAdoption #OrganizationalDesign #GenerativeAI #ManagementResearch

https://hbr.org/podcast/2026/05/microsof.centre_angle: This case documents how a large enterprise moved from near-zero habitual AI use to near-universal monthly adoption, and what managerial interventions were required to close that gap. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the Microsoft experience illustrates a recurring pattern: technical deployment is the easy part; the organizational and behavioral work of changing how thousands of employees actually operate is where most initiatives stall. Our Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index finds that the dominant barriers facing executives today are similarly organizational — siloed departments, cultural resistance, and the absence of visible quick wins — rather than anything technical. Microsoft's two-year arc is a large-scale test of what it takes to move past those barriers.
https://hbr.org/podcast/2026/05/microsof.claude_summary: Two years into deploying Microsoft Copilot across its 62,000-person sales organization, Microsoft reached daily active AI usage above 60% and monthly active usage above 98%. The path was not straightforward: six months after launch, adoption had stalled and the company had to rethink its deployment strategy based on early evidence. A Harvard Business School case study examines how Microsoft evolved its approach, what it took to shift entrenched working habits at scale, and what challenges remain as the organization moves toward integrating autonomous sales agents. The excerpt is drawn from a podcast discussion of the case and does not detail specific mechanisms or quantitative causal findings.
https://hbr.org/podcast/2026/05/microsof.key_takeaway: Getting to 98% monthly AI usage across a 62,000-person sales force took Microsoft two years of adaptive management, not just a product rollout — the organizational work dwarfed the technical work.
https://hbr.org/podcast/2026/05/microsof.linkedin_caption_draft:
Microsoft launched Copilot across 62,000 salespeople in 2023. Six months later, adoption had barely moved.

Two years on, daily active usage exceeds 60% and monthly usage tops 98%. The gap between those two data points is the real story — and it has almost nothing to do with the technology.

A new HBS case study traces the management decisions that closed the gap: how the company read early deployment signals, adjusted its approach, and eventually changed how tens of thousands of people actually work day to day. The harder problem now is integrating autonomous sales agents — where the stakes around accountability and workflow redesign are higher still.

CAMO's own research with Deloitte finds the same pattern across the region: AI adoption is nearly universal in intent among C-suite executives, but only 4% of firms describe themselves as fully operational. The bottleneck is organizational, not technical.

For leaders managing their own deployments: what did your early adoption data actually tell you — and did you act on it? 🔍

#AIAdoption #EnterpriseAI #ChangeManagement #FutureOfWork #CAMOHKU

https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.authors: Elizabeth M. Renieris, David Kiron, Steven Mills, and Anne Kleppe.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.centre_angle: This article argues that responsible AI governance has a blind spot: it evaluates models but not the workforce consequences of deploying them. From CAMO's perspective on AI and jobs, this gap is well-evidenced in practice — our research on the hidden competency penalty for AI users (see 'Research: The Hidden Penalty of Using AI at Work') shows that even when organizations provide AI access, the social and evaluative dynamics around its use can harm the very workers it is meant to help. Extending RAI frameworks to include job design, accountability attribution, and adoption incentives is not peripheral to responsible AI — it is central to whether deployment produces net benefit for organizations and the people inside them.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.claude_summary: A fifth consecutive annual survey by MIT Sloan Management Review and Boston Consulting Group examines how organizations are implementing responsible AI (RAI) worldwide. This year's edition shifts attention toward workforce impact as an underexamined dimension of RAI — arguing that responsible AI frameworks have focused heavily on model-level concerns (bias, explainability, safety) while neglecting the organizational and human consequences of deployment: job displacement, skill degradation, shifting accountability, and uneven distribution of AI's benefits and burdens. The article draws on an international panel of academics and practitioners to assess RAI maturity and calls on organizations to extend their governance frameworks to cover how AI reshapes work itself. Note: the full findings are behind a paywall; this summary is based on the RSS excerpt and title framing.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.key_takeaway: Responsible AI frameworks that stop at the model layer — ignoring how deployment reshapes jobs, accountability, and worker welfare — are incomplete governance, not just incomplete ethics.

matched_camo.reason: Both pieces identify a gap between organizational AI access/adoption policy and the social dynamics that determine whether employees can use AI without penalty — a direct illustration of why workforce impact belongs inside RAI frameworks.

The contrast between completed infrastructure below and empty, ungoverned space above carries the central argument.

https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.authors: Elizabeth M. Renieris, David Kiron, Steven Mills, and Anne Kleppe.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.centre_angle: This article argues that responsible AI requires organizations to move beyond procedural oversight toward cultivating genuine domain expertise capable of interrogating AI outputs. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, this points to an underappreciated organizational design problem: deploying AI is relatively tractable, but building the internal human capacity to hold AI accountable is a distinct and harder challenge. CAMO's work on the Dingdong VOC case illustrates this concretely—AI-driven attribution raised precision but created new demands on managerial judgment for edge cases, suggesting that responsible deployment redistributes, rather than eliminates, the burden on human experts.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.claude_summary: Drawing on five years of MIT SMR and BCG research on responsible AI implementation, this article argues that human oversight of AI systems cannot be reduced to verification—simply checking whether outputs are accurate or compliant. Instead, genuine responsibility requires human experts to exercise substantive judgment: understanding when AI recommendations are appropriate, when context changes the calculus, and when to override or escalate. The authors contend that organizations have overinvested in procedural compliance and underinvested in building the expert capacity needed to interrogate AI outputs meaningfully. Note: the full article is behind a paywall; the summary is based on the RSS excerpt and stated research framing.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.key_takeaway: Responsible AI demands organizations invest in expert judgment that can genuinely interrogate AI outputs—not just processes that create the appearance of oversight.
https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/beyo.linkedin_caption_draft:
Oversight is not the same as understanding.

A new MIT Sloan Management Review piece from a five-year BCG research collaboration argues that responsible AI has a structural gap: organizations have built compliance layers around AI systems, but have not invested equally in the expert capacity needed to interrogate those systems with genuine judgment.

Checking that outputs look correct is not the same as knowing when the question itself was wrong—or when a confident AI recommendation is operating outside the conditions it was designed for.

At CAMO, we see this dynamic at the organizational level. Our research on AI-driven performance evaluation at Dingdong found that automation raised precision but redistributed—rather than reduced—the burden on human judgment, particularly for contested attribution cases. The demand for expert oversight did not disappear; it moved.

For leaders designing AI governance, the question may be less 'do we have a review process?' and more 'do we have people capable of reviewing meaningfully?'

#ResponsibleAI #AIGovernance #AIAdoption #OrganizationalDesign #FutureOfWork

https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/st.centre_angle: This McKinsey interview raises the question of where AI-driven productivity in cognitive work actually accrues — to firms, workers, or neither. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the organizational challenge is not simply deploying AI but redesigning roles and incentive structures so that productivity gains translate into durable value rather than dissipating through coordination failures or eroded effort. CAMO's work on job design — including findings that AI can systematically shift firms toward lower-effort, lower-wage equilibria even when higher-effort configurations create more total surplus — suggests that 'capturing value at scale' depends as much on compensation architecture as on technical capability.
https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/st.claude_summary: Oxford economist Jean-Paul Carvalho, speaking with McKinsey, examines how AI is restructuring cognitive work and what that means for organizational strategy and business value creation. The excerpt is limited, but the framing centers on where AI creates leverage in knowledge-intensive tasks, how firms should position themselves to capture that value at scale, and what the redistribution of cognitive labor implies for workforce design. Because only the RSS excerpt is available, specific empirical findings cannot be confirmed; the conversation appears to be analytical and conceptual rather than data-driven.
https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/st.key_takeaway: Capturing value from AI in cognitive work is primarily an organizational and incentive design challenge, not a technical one.
https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/st.linkedin_caption_draft:
AI is reshaping cognitive work — but who actually captures the value?

Oxford economist Jean-Paul Carvalho's conversation with McKinsey puts the strategic question plainly: as AI absorbs more knowledge-intensive tasks, the firms that benefit are those that redesign their organizations around the new division of labor, not just the ones that deploy the tools fastest.

The catch is that organizational redesign is harder than technology adoption. CAMO's own survey of C-suite executives in China and Hong Kong found that nearly half reported AI initiatives fell short of expected ROI — and the barriers were almost entirely organizational: siloed teams, cultural resistance, and poorly sequenced implementation. Separately, CAMO research on job design suggests AI can quietly shift firms toward lower-effort role structures even when higher-effort configurations would create more total value.

The strategic question, then, is not 'how much AI can we deploy?' but 'what kind of organization are we building around it?'

How is your firm thinking about that second question? 🔍

#AIStrategy #FutureOfWork #OrganizationalDesign #AIAdoption #ManagementResearch

https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/indu.centre_angle: This McKinsey piece argues that scaling AI requires CEOs to redesign organizational structures, not simply expand technical pilots. From CAMO's perspective on AI adoption, the harder problem is not access to AI tools but the organizational and incentive frictions that prevent genuine use at scale — a pattern documented in our Deloitte-HKU AI Adoption Index, where nearly half of executives reported initiatives fell short of expected ROI, with siloed departments and cultural resistance as the leading culprits. The implication is that CEO attention to org-level redesign is necessary but insufficient without deliberate attention to how AI changes job boundaries, accountability, and the conditions under which workers actually adopt these tools.
https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/indu.claude_summary: McKinsey argues that AI's value creation potential has been demonstrated but remains difficult to scale across organizations. The central challenge for CEOs is moving from isolated experimentation to sustained, enterprise-wide performance improvement. The article frames this as a strategic and organizational design problem rather than a technical one: firms must reimagine structures, workflows, and operating models to capture AI's benefits systematically. The excerpt is thin on specific findings or data, so the precise mechanisms McKinsey recommends are not fully visible from the RSS summary alone. The overall argument is that AI deployment is now a CEO-level organizational transformation challenge, not a pilot program to be managed at the margin.
https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/indu.key_takeaway: Scaling AI across an organization is primarily an organizational design problem, and CEOs who treat it as a technical rollout are likely to leave most of the value on the table.
https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/indu.linkedin_caption_draft:
AI pilots succeed. Scaling them doesn't — at least not yet.

McKinsey's latest piece for CEOs makes a pointed argument: the bottleneck on AI value is no longer the technology, it's the organization. Reimagining structures, workflows, and operating models is now a CEO-level imperative, not a project to delegate.

CAMO's own survey of 100+ C-suite executives in China and Hong Kong found the same pattern: adoption is nearly universal, but fewer than 5% of firms describe themselves as fully transformational. The barriers are organizational — siloed departments, cultural resistance, and a lack of quick wins — not technical.

The question for executives isn't whether AI works in a controlled environment. It's whether the rest of the organization is designed to sustain what the pilot proved.

What structural change has been hardest to make in your AI rollout? 👇

#AIAdoption #OrganizationalDesign #EnterpriseAI #Leadership #FutureOfWork
